 15→/**
 16→ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 17→ *
 18→ * The date components are: [years], [months], [days].
 19→ *
 20→ * The time components are: [hours], [minutes], [seconds], [nanoseconds].
 21→ *
 22→ * A `DateTimePeriod` can be constructed using the same-named constructor function,
 23→ * [parsed][DateTimePeriod.parse] from a string, or returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 24→ * All these functions can return a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of `DateTimePeriod`,
 25→ * a special case that only stores date components, if all time components of the result happen to be zero.
 26→ */
 27→@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 28→// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 29→public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 30→    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 31→
 32→    /**
 33→     * The number of calendar days.
 34→     *
 35→     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 36→     */
 37→    public abstract val days: Int
 38→    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 39→
 40→    /**
 41→     * The number of whole years.
 42→     */
 43→    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 44→
 45→    /**
 46→     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 47→     */
 48→    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
 49→
 50→    /**
 51→     * The number of whole hours in this period.
 52→     */
 53→    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
 54→
 55→    /**
 56→     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 57→     */
 58→    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
 59→
 60→    /**
 61→     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 62→     */
 63→    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 64→
 65→    /**
 66→     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
 67→     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
 68→     */
 69→    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 70→
 71→    private fun allNonpositive() =
 72→        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
 73→
 74→    /**
 75→     * Converts this period to the ISO-8601 string representation for durations.
 76→     *
 77→     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
 78→     */
 79→    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
 80→        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
 81→        append('P')
 82→        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
 83→        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
 84→        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
 85→        var t = "T"
 86→        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
 87→        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
 88→        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
 89→            append(t)
 90→            append(when {
 91→                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
 92→                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
 93→                else -> "0"
 94→            })
 95→            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
 96→            append('S')
 97→        }
 98→
 99→        if (length == 1) append("0D")
100→    }
101→
102→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
103→        if (this === other) return true
104→        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
105→
106→        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
107→        if (days != other.days) return false
108→        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
109→
110→        return true
111→    }
112→
113→    override fun hashCode(): Int {
114→        var result = totalMonths
115→        result = 31 * result + days
116→        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
117→        return result
118→    }
119→
120→    public companion object {
121→        /**
122→         * Parses a ISO-8601 duration string as a [DateTimePeriod].
123→         * If the time components are absent or equal to zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
124→         *
125→         * Additionally, we support the `W` signifier to represent weeks.
126→         *
127→         * Examples of durations in the ISO-8601 format:
128→         * - `P1Y40D` is one year and 40 days
129→         * - `-P1DT1H` is minus (one day and one hour)
130→         * - `P1DT-1H` is one day minus one hour
131→         * - `-PT0.000000001S` is minus one nanosecond
132→         *
133→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [DateTimePeriod] are
134→         * exceeded.
135→         */
136→        public fun parse(text: String): DateTimePeriod {
137→            fun parseException(message: String, position: Int): Nothing =
138→                throw DateTimeFormatException("Parse error at char $position: $message")
139→            val START = 0
140→            val AFTER_P = 1
141→            val AFTER_YEAR = 2
142→            val AFTER_MONTH = 3
143→            val AFTER_WEEK = 4
144→            val AFTER_DAY = 5
145→            val AFTER_T = 6
146→            val AFTER_HOUR = 7
147→            val AFTER_MINUTE = 8
148→            val AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO = 9
149→
150→            var state = START
151→            // next unread character
152→            var i = 0
153→            var sign = 1
154→            var years = 0
155→            var months = 0
156→            var weeks = 0
157→            var days = 0
158→            var hours = 0
159→            var minutes = 0
160→            var seconds = 0
161→            var nanoseconds = 0
162→            while (true) {
163→                if (i >= text.length) {
164→                    if (state == START)
165→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; 'P' designator is required", i)
166→                    if (state == AFTER_T)
167→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; at least one time component is required after 'T'", i)
168→                    val daysTotal = when (val n = days.toLong() + weeks * 7) {
169→                        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> n.toInt()
170→                        else -> parseException("The total number of days under 'D' and 'W' designators should fit into an Int", 0)
171→                    }
172→                    return DateTimePeriod(years, months, daysTotal, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds.toLong())
173→                }
174→                if (state == START) {
175→                    if (i + 1 >= text.length && (text[i] == '+' || text[i] == '-'))
176→                        parseException("Unexpected end of string; 'P' designator is required", i)
177→                    when (text[i]) {
178→                        '+', '-' -> {
179→                            if (text[i] == '-')
180→                                sign = -1
181→                            if (text[i + 1] != 'P')
182→                                parseException("Expected 'P', got '${text[i + 1]}'", i + 1)
183→                            i += 2
184→                        }
185→                        'P' -> { i += 1 }
186→                        else -> parseException("Expected '+', '-', 'P', got '${text[i]}'", i)
187→                    }
188→                    state = AFTER_P
189→                    continue
190→                }
191→                var localSign = sign
192→                val iStart = i
193→                when (text[i]) {
194→                    '+', '-' -> {
195→                        if (text[i] == '-') localSign *= -1
196→                        i += 1
197→                        if (i >= text.length || text[i] !in '0'..'9')
198→                            parseException("A number expected after '${text[i]}'", i)
199→                    }
200→                    in '0'..'9' -> { }
201→                    'T' -> {
202→                        if (state >= AFTER_T)
203→                            parseException("Only one 'T' designator is allowed", i)
204→                        state = AFTER_T
205→                        i += 1
206→                        continue
207→                    }
208→                }
209→                var number = 0L
210→                while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9') {
211→                    try {
212→                        number = safeAdd(safeMultiply(number, 10), (text[i] - '0').toLong())
213→                    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
214→                        parseException("The number is too large", iStart)
215→                    }
216→                    i += 1
217→                }
218→                number *= localSign
219→                if (i == text.length)
220→                    parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
221→                val wrongOrder = "Wrong component order: should be 'Y', 'M', 'W', 'D', then designator 'T', then 'H', 'M', 'S'"
222→                fun Long.toIntThrowing(component: Char): Int {
223→                    if (this < Int.MIN_VALUE || this > Int.MAX_VALUE)
224→                        parseException("Value $this does not fit into an Int, which is required for component '$component'", iStart)
225→                    return toInt()
226→                }
227→                when (text[i].uppercaseChar()) {
228→                    'Y' -> {
229→                        if (state >= AFTER_YEAR)
230→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
231→                        state = AFTER_YEAR
232→                        years = number.toIntThrowing('Y')
233→                    }
234→                    'M' -> {
235→                        if (state >= AFTER_T) {
236→                            // Minutes
237→                            if (state >= AFTER_MINUTE)
238→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
239→                            state = AFTER_MINUTE
240→                            minutes = number.toIntThrowing('M')
241→                        } else {
242→                            // Months
243→                            if (state >= AFTER_MONTH)
244→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
245→                            state = AFTER_MONTH
246→                            months = number.toIntThrowing('M')
247→                        }
248→                    }
249→                    'W' -> {
250→                        if (state >= AFTER_WEEK)
251→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
252→                        state = AFTER_WEEK
253→                        weeks = number.toIntThrowing('W')
254→                    }
255→                    'D' -> {
256→                        if (state >= AFTER_DAY)
257→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
258→                        state = AFTER_DAY
259→                        days = number.toIntThrowing('D')
260→                    }
261→                    'H' -> {
262→                        if (state >= AFTER_HOUR || state < AFTER_T)
263→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
264→                        state = AFTER_HOUR
265→                        hours = number.toIntThrowing('H')
266→                    }
267→                    'S' -> {
268→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
269→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
270→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
271→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
272→                    }
273→                    '.', ',' -> {
274→                        i += 1
275→                        if (i >= text.length)
276→                            parseException("Expected designator 'S' after ${text[i - 1]}", i)
277→                        val iStartFraction = i
278→                        while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9')
279→                            i += 1
280→                        val fractionLength = i - iStartFraction
281→                        if (fractionLength > 9)
282→                            parseException("Only the nanosecond fractions of a second are supported", iStartFraction)
283→                        val fractionalPart = text.substring(iStartFraction, i) + "0".repeat(9 - fractionLength)
284→                        nanoseconds = fractionalPart.toInt(10) * localSign
285→                        if (text[i] != 'S')
286→                            parseException("Expected the 'S' designator after a fraction", i)
287→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
288→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
289→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
290→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
291→                    }
292→                    else -> parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
293→                }
294→                i += 1
295→           }
296→        }
297→    }
298→}

301→ * @suppress
302→ */
303→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DateTimePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
304→public fun String.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = DateTimePeriod.parse(this)

306→/**
307→ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores date components and has all time components equal to zero.
308→ *
309→ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
310→ * the time components are zero.
311→ *
312→ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate],
313→ * but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
314→ */
315→@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
316→public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
317→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
318→    override val days: Int,
319→) : DateTimePeriod() {
320→    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
321→    // avoiding excessive computations
322→    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
323→    override val hours: Int get() = 0
324→
325→    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
326→    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
327→
328→    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
329→    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
330→
331→    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
332→    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
333→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0
334→
335→    public companion object {
336→        /**
337→         * Parses the ISO-8601 duration representation as a [DatePeriod].
338→         *
339→         * This function is equivalent to [DateTimePeriod.parse], but will fail if any of the time components are not
340→         * zero.
341→         *
342→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed, the boundaries of [DatePeriod] are exceeded,
343→         * or any time components are not zero.
344→         *
345→         * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
346→         */
347→        public fun parse(text: String): DatePeriod =
348→            when (val period = DateTimePeriod.parse(text)) {
349→                is DatePeriod -> period
350→                else -> throw DateTimeFormatException("Period $period (parsed from string $text) is not date-based")
351→            }
352→    }
353→}

355→/**
356→ * @suppress
357→ */
358→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DatePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
359→public fun String.toDatePeriod(): DatePeriod = DatePeriod.parse(this)

361→private class DateTimePeriodImpl(
362→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
363→    override val days: Int,
364→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long,
365→) : DateTimePeriod()
366→
367→// TODO: these calculations fit in a JS Number. Possible to do an expect/actual here.
368→private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
369→    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
370→        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
371→        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
372→    }
373→
374→private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
375→    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
376→    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
377→    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
378→    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
379→    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
380→    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
381→    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
382→    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
383→    return try {
384→        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
385→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
386→        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
387→    }
388→}
389→
390→internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
391→    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)
392→        DateTimePeriodImpl(totalMonths, days, totalNanoseconds)
393→    else
394→        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)
395→
396→/**
397→ * Constructs a new [DateTimePeriod]. If all the time components are zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
398→ *
399→ * It is recommended to always explicitly name the arguments when constructing this manually,
400→ * like `DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 12)`.
401→ *
402→ * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
403→ * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2)`.
404→ *
405→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
406→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [hours], [minutes], [seconds] and [nanoseconds]
407→ * overflows a [Long].
408→ */
409→public fun DateTimePeriod(
410→    years: Int = 0,
411→    months: Int = 0,
412→    days: Int = 0,
413→    hours: Int = 0,
414→    minutes: Int = 0,
415→    seconds: Int = 0,
416→    nanoseconds: Long = 0
417→): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths(years, months), days,
418→    totalNanoseconds(hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds))
419→
420→/**
421→ * Constructs a [DateTimePeriod] from a [Duration].
422→ *
423→ * If the duration value is too big to be represented as a [Long] number of nanoseconds,
424→ * the result will be [Long.MAX_VALUE] nanoseconds.
425→ */
426→// TODO: maybe it's more consistent to throw here on overflow?
427→public fun Duration.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalNanoseconds = inWholeNanoseconds)
428→
429→/**
430→ * Adds two [DateTimePeriod] instances.
431→ *
432→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
433→ */
434→public operator fun DateTimePeriod.plus(other: DateTimePeriod): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(
435→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
436→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
437→    safeAdd(totalNanoseconds, other.totalNanoseconds),
438→)
439→
440→/**
441→ * Adds two [DatePeriod] instances.
442→ *
443→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
444→ */
445→public operator fun DatePeriod.plus(other: DatePeriod): DatePeriod = DatePeriod(
446→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
447→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
448→)

 14→/**
 15→ * A unit for measuring time.
 16→ *
 17→ * See the predefined constants for time units, like [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.DAY],
 18→ * [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], and others.
 19→ *
 20→ * Two ways are provided to create custom [DateTimeUnit] instances:
 21→ * - By multiplying an existing unit on the right by an integer scalar: for example, `DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 10`.
 22→ * - By constructing an instance manually with [TimeBased], [DayBased], or [MonthBased]: for example,
 23→ *   `TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10)`.
 24→ *
 25→ * Note that a calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours. See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for a discussion.
 26→ */
 27→@Serializable(with = DateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 28→public sealed class DateTimeUnit {
 29→
 30→    /** Produces a date-time unit that is a multiple of this unit times the specified integer [scalar] value. */
 31→    public abstract operator fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit
 32→
 33→    /**
 34→     * A date-time unit that has the precise time duration.
 35→     *
 36→     * Such units are independent of the time zone.
 37→     * Any such unit can be represented as some fixed number of nanoseconds.
 38→     */
 39→    @Serializable(with = TimeBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 40→    public class TimeBased(
 41→        /**
 42→         * The length of this unit in nanoseconds.
 43→         */
 44→        public val nanoseconds: Long
 45→    ) : DateTimeUnit() {
 46→        private val unitName: String
 47→        private val unitScale: Long
 48→
 49→        init {
 50→            require(nanoseconds > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $nanoseconds ns." }
 51→            // find a concise string representation for the unit with this duration
 52→            when {
 53→                nanoseconds % 3600_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 54→                    unitName = "HOUR"
 55→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 3600_000_000_000
 56→                }
 57→                nanoseconds % 60_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 58→                    unitName = "MINUTE"
 59→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 60_000_000_000
 60→                }
 61→                nanoseconds % 1_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 62→                    unitName = "SECOND"
 63→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000_000
 64→                }
 65→                nanoseconds % 1_000_000 == 0L -> {
 66→                    unitName = "MILLISECOND"
 67→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000
 68→                }
 69→                nanoseconds % 1_000 == 0L -> {
 70→                    unitName = "MICROSECOND"
 71→                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000
 72→                }
 73→                else -> {
 74→                    unitName = "NANOSECOND"
 75→                    unitScale = nanoseconds
 76→                }
 77→            }
 78→        }
 79→
 80→        override fun times(scalar: Int): TimeBased = TimeBased(safeMultiply(nanoseconds, scalar.toLong()))
 81→
 82→        /**
 83→         * The length of this unit as a [Duration].
 84→         */
 85→        public val duration: Duration
 86→            get() = nanoseconds.nanoseconds
 87→
 88→        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 89→            this === other || (other is TimeBased && this.nanoseconds == other.nanoseconds)
 90→
 91→        override fun hashCode(): Int = nanoseconds.toInt() xor (nanoseconds shr Int.SIZE_BITS).toInt()
 92→
 93→        override fun toString(): String = formatToString(unitScale, unitName)
 94→    }

 96→    /**
 97→     * A date-time unit equal to some number of days or months.
 98→     *
 99→     * Operations involving `DateBased` units are performed on dates. The same operations on [Instants][Instant]
100→     * require a [TimeZone] to find the corresponding [LocalDateTimes][LocalDateTime] first to perform
101→     * the operation with the date component of these `LocalDateTime` values.

104→    public sealed class DateBased : DateTimeUnit() {
105→        @Suppress("TOPLEVEL_TYPEALIASES_ONLY")
106→        @Deprecated("Use DateTimeUnit.DayBased", ReplaceWith("DateTimeUnit.DayBased", "kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeUnit"))
107→        public typealias DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased
108→        @Suppress("TOPLEVEL_TYPEALIASES_ONLY")
109→        @Deprecated("Use DateTimeUnit.MonthBased", ReplaceWith("DateTimeUnit.MonthBased", "kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeUnit"))
110→        public typealias MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased

113→    /**
114→     * A date-time unit equal to some number of calendar days.
115→     *
116→     * A calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours, thus a `DayBased`-unit cannot be expressed as a multiple of some [TimeBased]-unit.
117→     *
118→     * The reason lies in time zone transitions, because of which some days can be 23 or 25 hours.
119→     * For example, we say that exactly a whole day has passed between `2019-10-27T02:59` and `2019-10-28T02:59`
120→     * in Berlin, despite the fact that the clocks were turned back one hour, so there are, in fact, 25 hours
121→     * between the two date-times.

123→    @Serializable(with = DayBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
124→    public class DayBased(
125→        /**
126→         * The length of this unit in days.
127→         */
128→        public val days: Int
129→    ) : DateBased() {

131→            require(days > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $days days." }
132→        }
133→
134→        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased(safeMultiply(days, scalar))
135→
136→        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
137→            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.DayBased && this.days == other.days)
138→
139→        override fun hashCode(): Int = days xor 0x10000
140→
141→        override fun toString(): String = if (days % 7 == 0)

148→     * A date-time unit equal to some number of months.

152→    @Serializable(with = MonthBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
153→    public class MonthBased(

161→        }

163→        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased(safeMultiply(months, scalar))
164→
165→        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
166→            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased && this.months == other.months)

170→        override fun toString(): String = when {
171→            months % 12_00 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12_00, "CENTURY")

173→            months % 3 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 3, "QUARTER")
174→            else -> formatToString(months, "MONTH")

176→    }

178→    protected fun formatToString(value: Int, unit: String): String = if (value == 1) unit else "$value-$unit"
179→    protected fun formatToString(value: Long, unit: String): String = if (value == 1L) unit else "$value-$unit"

181→    public companion object {
182→        /**
183→         * A nanosecond, which is `1/1_000_000_000` of a second.

185→        public val NANOSECOND: TimeBased = TimeBased(nanoseconds = 1)

188→         * A microsecond, which is `1/1_000_000` of a second, or `1_000` nanoseconds.
189→         */
190→        public val MICROSECOND: TimeBased = NANOSECOND * 1000

229→        public val MONTH: MonthBased = MonthBased(months = 1)

234→        public val QUARTER: MonthBased = MONTH * 3
235→
236→        /**
237→         * A year, which is 12 [months][MONTH].

240→
241→        /**

  9→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer

 14→/**
 15→ * A moment in time.
 16→ *
 17→ * This class represents a specific point in time, independent of any time zone or calendar.
 18→ * It corresponds to the `Instant` type in `java.time` and is compatible with it on the JVM.
 19→ *
 20→ * `Instant` is the main type for storing timestamps, such as log entry times,
 21→ * transaction times, or any other event that happens at a specific moment.
 22→ *
 23→ * ### Time scale
 24→ *
 25→ * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale.
 26→ * This time scale doesn't contain instants corresponding to leap seconds,
 27→ * but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 28→ * of the day when a leap second happens.
 29→ * This means that for any day, the time 23:59:59 is valid and is followed by 00:00:00 of the next day.
 30→ *
 31→ * This simplifies calculations, as every day is exactly 86400 seconds long.
 32→ * However, it means that `Instant` cannot represent the exact moment of a leap second.
 33→ *
 34→ * ### Acquiring an Instant
 35→ *
 36→ * There are several ways to obtain an `Instant`:
 37→ * - [Clock.now]: queries the current moment from a clock.
 38→ *   ```
 39→ *   val now = Clock.System.now()
 40→ *   ```
 41→ * - [parse]: parses an ISO-8601 string.
 42→ *   ```
 43→ *   val instant = Instant.parse("2023-01-02T12:34:56Z")
 44→ *   ```
 45→ * - [fromEpochMilliseconds] and [fromEpochSeconds]: constructs an instant from a Unix timestamp.
 46→ *   ```
 47→ *   val instant = Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(1672662896000L)
 48→ *   ```
 49→ *
 50→ * ### Using an Instant
 51→ *
 52→ * - **Comparison**: Instants can be compared using standard operators (`<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`).
 53→ * - **Arithmetic**: You can add or subtract a [Duration] to get another `Instant`.
 54→ *   ```
 55→ *   val later = now + 1.hours
 56→ *   ```
 57→ * - **Calendar Arithmetic**: To add calendar units (like months or years), you need a [TimeZone],
 58→ *   because the length of a month or year depends on the time zone rules (e.g. DST transitions).
 59→ *   ```
 60→ *   val nextMonth = now.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.MONTH, TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 61→ *   ```
 62→ * - **Conversion**: Convert to [LocalDateTime] using a [TimeZone] to see the date and time components.
 63→ *   ```
 64→ *   val dateTime = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 65→ *   ```
 66→ *
 67→ * ### Platform specifics
 68→ *
 69→ * - **JVM**: Wraps `java.time.Instant`.
 70→ * - **JS**: Wraps `Date` or `js-joda`'s `Instant`.
 71→ * - **Native**: Uses a custom implementation based on the `date` C++ library.
 72→ *
 73→ * The range of supported values is platform-dependent but is guaranteed to cover at least the range
 74→ * from [DISTANT_PAST] to [DISTANT_FUTURE].
 75→ *
 76→ * @see LocalDateTime
 77→ * @see TimeZone
 78→ * @see Clock
 79→ */
 80→@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 81→public expect class Instant : Comparable<Instant> {
 82→
 83→    /**
 84→     * The number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` rounded down to a [Long] number.
 85→     *
 86→     * The difference between the rounded number of seconds and the actual number of seconds
 87→     * is returned by [nanosecondsOfSecond] property expressed in nanoseconds.
 88→     *
 89→     * Note that this number doesn't include leap seconds added or removed since the epoch.
 90→     *
 91→     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 92→     */
 93→    public val epochSeconds: Long
 94→
 95→    /**
 96→     * The number of nanoseconds by which this instant is later than [epochSeconds] from the epoch instant.
 97→     *
 98→     * The value is always positive and lies in the range `0..999_999_999`.
 99→     *
100→     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
101→     */
102→    public val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
103→
104→    /**
105→     * Returns the number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
106→     *
107→     * Any fractional part of millisecond is rounded down to the whole number of milliseconds.
108→     *
109→     * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
110→     *
111→     * @see Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds
112→     */
113→    public fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long
114→
115→    /**
116→     * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the specified [duration] to this instant.
117→     *
118→     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
119→     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
120→     *
121→     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
122→     *
123→     * Note that this operation adds physical time, not calendar time.
124→     * Adding `24.hours` to an instant always results in an instant exactly 24 hours later,
125→     * regardless of day length changes due to daylight saving time.
126→     * If you want to add calendar units (like days, months, or years) respecting time zone rules,
127→     * use [plus] with [DateTimeUnit] and [TimeZone].
128→     */
129→    public operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant
130→
131→    /**
132→     * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the specified [duration] from this instant.
133→     *
134→     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
135→     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
136→     *
137→     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
138→     *
139→     * Note that this operation subtracts physical time, not calendar time.
140→     * Subtracting `24.hours` from an instant always results in an instant exactly 24 hours earlier,
141→     * regardless of day length changes due to daylight saving time.
142→     * If you want to subtract calendar units (like days, months, or years) respecting time zone rules,
143→     * use [minus] with [DateTimeUnit] and [TimeZone].
144→     */
145→    public operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant
146→
147→    // questionable
148→    /**
149→     * Returns the [Duration] between two instants: [other] and `this`.
150→     *
151→     * The duration returned is positive if this instant is later than the other,
152→     * and negative if this instant is earlier than the other.
153→     *
154→     * The result is never clamped, but note that for instants that are far apart,
155→     * the value returned may represent the duration between them inexactly due to the loss of precision.
156→     */
157→    public operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration
158→
159→    /**

161→     * Returns zero if this instant represents the same moment as the other (i.e. equal to other),
162→     * a negative number if this instant is earlier than the other,
163→     * and a positive number if this instant is later than the other.
164→     */
165→    public override operator fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int
166→
167→    /**
168→     * Converts this instant to the ISO-8601 string representation.

170→     * The representation uses the UTC-SLS time scale, instead of UTC.
171→     * In practice, this means that leap second handling will not be readjusted to the UTC.
172→     * Leap seconds will not be added or skipped, so it is impossible to acquire a string
173→     * where the component for seconds is 60, and for any day, it's possible to observe 23:59:59.
174→     *
175→     * Examples:

181→     * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] will not add trailing zeros for readability to the
182→     * fractional part of the second.
183→     */
184→    public override fun toString(): String
185→
186→
187→    public companion object {
188→        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
189→        public fun now(): Instant

191→        /**
192→         * Returns an [Instant] that is [epochMilliseconds] number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
193→         *

195→         *
196→         * @see Instant.toEpochMilliseconds
197→         */
198→        public fun fromEpochMilliseconds(epochMilliseconds: Long): Instant
199→
200→        /**
201→         * Returns an [Instant] that is the [epochSeconds] number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`
202→         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
203→         *
204→         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
205→         */
206→        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long = 0): Instant
207→
208→        /**
209→         * Returns an [Instant] that is the [epochSeconds] number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`
210→         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
211→         *
212→         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
213→         */
214→        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Int): Instant
215→
216→        /**
217→         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse], followed by [DateTimeComponents.toInstantUsingOffset].
218→         *
219→         * Parses a string that represents an instant including date and time components and a mandatory
220→         * time zone offset and returns the parsed [Instant] value.
221→         *
222→         * The string is considered to represent time on the UTC-SLS time scale instead of UTC.
223→         * In practice, this means that, even if there is a leap second on the given day, it will not affect how the
224→         * time is parsed, even if it's in the last 1000 seconds of the day.
225→         * Instead, even if there is a negative leap second on the given day, 23:59:59 is still considered valid time.
226→         * 23:59:60 is invalid on UTC-SLS, so parsing it will fail.
227→         *
228→         * If the format is not specified, [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is used.
229→         *
230→         * Examples:
231→         * - `Instant.parse("2023-01-02T12:34:56Z")`
232→         * - `Instant.parse("2023-01-02T12:34:56.123Z")`
233→         * - `Instant.parse("2023-01-02T14:34:56+02:00")`

235→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [Instant] are exceeded.
236→         *

249→         * [LocalDateTime] without exceptions on all supported platforms.
250→         */
251→        public val DISTANT_PAST: Instant // -100001-12-31T23:59:59.999999999Z

253→        /**

258→         */
259→        public val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant // +100000-01-01T00:00:00Z

262→        internal val MAX: Instant
263→    }

266→/** Returns true if the instant is [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] or earlier. */

268→    get() = this <= Instant.DISTANT_PAST

282→ * added in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to nanoseconds.

285→ * the result depends on the [timeZone].

288→ * [LocalDateTime].

292→/**

294→ * are subtracted in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to nanoseconds.

297→ * [LocalDateTime].

299→public fun Instant.minus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
300→    /* An overflow can happen for any component, but we are only worried about nanoseconds, as having an overflow in
301→    any other component means that `plus` will throw due to the minimum value of the numeric type overflowing the
302→    platform-specific limits. */
303→    if (period.totalNanoseconds != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
304→        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -totalNanoseconds) }
305→        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone)
306→    } else {
307→        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -(totalNanoseconds+1)) }
308→        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone).plus(1, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND)

418→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].

420→@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit, timeZone)"))
421→public expect fun Instant.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
422→
423→/**
424→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting one [unit] from this instant
425→ * in the specified [timeZone].
426→ *
427→ * The returned instant is earlier than this instant.
428→ *
429→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
430→ */
431→@Deprecated("Use the minus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.minus(1, unit, timeZone)"))
432→public fun Instant.minus(unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
433→    plus(-1, unit, timeZone)
434→
435→/**
436→ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding one [unit] to this instant.
437→ *
438→ * The returned instant is later than this instant.
439→ *
440→ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
441→ */
442→@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit)"))
443→public fun Instant.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
444→    plus(1L, unit)
445→
446→/**
447→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting one [unit] from this instant.
448→ *
449→ * The returned instant is earlier than this instant.
450→ *
451→ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
452→ */
453→@Deprecated("Use the minus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.minus(1, unit)"))
454→public fun Instant.minus(unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
455→    plus(-1L, unit)
456→
457→/**
458→ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this instant
459→ * in the specified [timeZone].
460→ *
461→ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
462→ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
463→ *
464→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
465→ */
466→public expect fun Instant.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
467→
468→/**
469→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this instant
470→ * in the specified [timeZone].
471→ *
472→ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
473→ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
474→ *
475→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
476→ */
477→public expect fun Instant.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
478→
479→/**
480→ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this instant.
481→ *
482→ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
483→ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
484→ *
485→ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
486→ */
487→public fun Instant.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
488→    plus(value.toLong(), unit)
489→
490→/**
491→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this instant.
492→ *
493→ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
494→ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
495→ *
496→ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
497→ */
498→public fun Instant.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
499→    minus(value.toLong(), unit)
500→
501→/**
502→ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this instant
503→ * in the specified [timeZone].
504→ *
505→ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
506→ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
507→ *
508→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
509→ */
510→public expect fun Instant.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
511→
512→/**
513→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this instant
514→ * in the specified [timeZone].
515→ *
516→ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
517→ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
518→ *
519→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
520→ */
521→public fun Instant.minus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
522→    if (value != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
523→        plus(-value, unit, timeZone)
524→    } else {
525→        plus(-(value + 1), unit, timeZone).plus(1, unit, timeZone)
526→    }
527→
528→/**
529→ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this instant.
530→ *
531→ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
532→ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
533→ *
534→ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
535→ */
536→public expect fun Instant.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant
537→
538→/**
539→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this instant.
540→ *
541→ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
542→ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
543→ *
544→ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
545→ */
546→public fun Instant.minus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
547→    if (value != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
548→        plus(-value, unit)
549→    } else {
550→        plus(-(value + 1), unit).plus(1, unit)
551→    }
552→
553→/**
554→ * Returns the whole number of the specified date or time [units][unit] between [other] and `this` instants
555→ * in the specified [timeZone].
556→ *
557→ * The value returned is negative or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
558→ * and positive or zero if this instant is later than the other.
559→ *
560→ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
561→ *
562→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
563→ * @see Instant.until
564→ */
565→public fun Instant.minus(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long =
566→        other.until(this, unit, timeZone)
567→
568→/**
569→ * Returns the whole number of the specified time [units][unit] between [other] and `this` instants.
570→ *
571→ * The value returned is negative or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
572→ * and positive or zero if this instant is later than the other.
573→ *
574→ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
575→ *
576→ * @see Instant.until
577→ */
578→public fun Instant.minus(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Long =
579→    other.until(this, unit)

581→/**
582→ * Formats this value using the given [format] using the given [offset].
583→ *
584→ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] and using [DateTimeComponents.setDateTimeOffset] in
585→ * the lambda.
586→ *
587→ * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is a format very similar to the one used by [toString].
588→ * The only difference is that [Instant.toString] adds trailing zeros to the fraction-of-second component so that the
589→ * number of digits after a dot is a multiple of three.
590→ */
591→public fun Instant.format(format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>, offset: UtcOffset = UtcOffset.ZERO): String {
592→    val instant = this
593→    return format.format { setDateTimeOffset(instant, offset) }
594→}

  6→package kotlinx.datetime
  7→
  8→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DatePeriodIso8601Serializer
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer
 11→import kotlin.math.*
 12→import kotlin.time.Duration
 13→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 14→
 15→/**
 16→ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 17→ *
 18→ * This class represents a period of time in terms of calendar fields (years, months, days)
 19→ * and time fields (hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds).
 20→ *
 21→ * It is often used to represent the result of calculating the difference between two Instants
 22→ * (e.g. "2 years, 3 months, and 5 days").
 23→ *
 24→ * ### Components
 25→ *
 26→ * The date components are: [years], [months], [days].
 27→ * The time components are: [hours], [minutes], [seconds], [nanoseconds].
 28→ *
 29→ * Note that the components are normalized. For example, `DateTimePeriod(months = 13)`
 30→ * will result in a period with 1 year and 1 month.
 31→ *
 32→ * ### Acquiring a DateTimePeriod
 33→ *
 34→ * - [parse]: Parses an ISO-8601 string (e.g. `P1Y2M3DT4H`).
 35→ *   ```
 36→ *   val period = DateTimePeriod.parse("P1Y2M")
 37→ *   ```
 38→ * - Constructor function:
 39→ *   ```
 40→ *   val period = DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 2)
 41→ *   ```
 42→ * - Difference between instants:
 43→ *   ```
 44→ *   val period = instant1.periodUntil(instant2, timeZone)
 45→ *   ```
 46→ *
 47→ * @see DatePeriod
 48→ */
 49→@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 50→// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 51→public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 52→    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 53→
 54→    /**
 55→     * The number of calendar days.
 56→     *
 57→     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 58→     */
 59→    public abstract val days: Int
 60→    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 61→
 62→    /**
 63→     * The number of whole years.
 64→     */
 65→    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 66→
 67→    /**
 68→     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 69→     */
 70→    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
 71→
 72→    /**
 73→     * The number of whole hours in this period.
 74→     */
 75→    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
 76→
 77→    /**
 78→     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 79→     */
 80→    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
 81→
 82→    /**
 83→     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 84→     */
 85→    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 86→
 87→    /**
 88→     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
 89→     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
 90→     */
 91→    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 92→
 93→    private fun allNonpositive() =
 94→        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
 95→
 96→    /**
 97→     * Converts this period to the ISO-8601 string representation for durations.
 98→     *
 99→     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
100→     */
101→    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
102→        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
103→        append('P')
104→        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
105→        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
106→        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
107→        var t = "T"
108→        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
109→        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
110→        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
111→            append(t)
112→            append(when {
113→                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
114→                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
115→                else -> "0"
116→            })
117→            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
118→            append('S')
119→        }
120→
121→        if (length == 1) append("0D")
122→    }
123→
124→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
125→        if (this === other) return true
126→        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
127→
128→        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
129→        if (days != other.days) return false
130→        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
131→
132→        return true
133→    }
134→
135→    override fun hashCode(): Int {
136→        var result = totalMonths
137→        result = 31 * result + days
138→        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
139→        return result
140→    }
141→
142→    public companion object {
143→        /**
144→         * Parses a ISO-8601 duration string as a [DateTimePeriod].
145→         * If the time components are absent or equal to zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
146→         *
147→         * Additionally, we support the `W` signifier to represent weeks.
148→         *
149→         * Examples of durations in the ISO-8601 format:
150→         * - `P1Y40D` is one year and 40 days
151→         * - `-P1DT1H` is minus (one day and one hour)
152→         * - `P1DT-1H` is one day minus one hour
153→         * - `-PT0.000000001S` is minus one nanosecond
154→         *
155→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [DateTimePeriod] are
156→         * exceeded.
157→         */
158→        public fun parse(text: String): DateTimePeriod {
159→            fun parseException(message: String, position: Int): Nothing =
160→                throw DateTimeFormatException("Parse error at char $position: $message")
161→            val START = 0
162→            val AFTER_P = 1
163→            val AFTER_YEAR = 2
164→            val AFTER_MONTH = 3
165→            val AFTER_WEEK = 4
166→            val AFTER_DAY = 5
167→            val AFTER_T = 6
168→            val AFTER_HOUR = 7
169→            val AFTER_MINUTE = 8
170→            val AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO = 9
171→
172→            var state = START
173→            // next unread character
174→            var i = 0
175→            var sign = 1
176→            var years = 0
177→            var months = 0
178→            var weeks = 0
179→            var days = 0
180→            var hours = 0
181→            var minutes = 0
182→            var seconds = 0
183→            var nanoseconds = 0
184→            while (true) {
185→                if (i >= text.length) {
186→                    if (state == START)
187→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; 'P' designator is required", i)
188→                    if (state == AFTER_T)
189→                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; at least one time component is required after 'T'", i)
190→                    val daysTotal = when (val n = days.toLong() + weeks * 7) {
191→                        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> n.toInt()
192→                        else -> parseException("The total number of days under 'D' and 'W' designators should fit into an Int", 0)
193→                    }
194→                    return DateTimePeriod(years, months, daysTotal, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds.toLong())
195→                }
196→                if (state == START) {
197→                    if (i + 1 >= text.length && (text[i] == '+' || text[i] == '-'))
198→                        parseException("Unexpected end of string; 'P' designator is required", i)
199→                    when (text[i]) {
200→                        '+', '-' -> {
201→                            if (text[i] == '-')
202→                                sign = -1
203→                            if (text[i + 1] != 'P')
204→                                parseException("Expected 'P', got '${text[i + 1]}'", i + 1)
205→                            i += 2
206→                        }
207→                        'P' -> { i += 1 }
208→                        else -> parseException("Expected '+', '-', 'P', got '${text[i]}'", i)
209→                    }
210→                    state = AFTER_P
211→                    continue
212→                }
213→                var localSign = sign
214→                val iStart = i
215→                when (text[i]) {
216→                    '+', '-' -> {
217→                        if (text[i] == '-') localSign *= -1
218→                        i += 1
219→                        if (i >= text.length || text[i] !in '0'..'9')
220→                            parseException("A number expected after '${text[i]}'", i)
221→                    }
222→                    in '0'..'9' -> { }
223→                    'T' -> {
224→                        if (state >= AFTER_T)
225→                            parseException("Only one 'T' designator is allowed", i)
226→                        state = AFTER_T
227→                        i += 1
228→                        continue
229→                    }
230→                }
231→                var number = 0L
232→                while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9') {
233→                    try {
234→                        number = safeAdd(safeMultiply(number, 10), (text[i] - '0').toLong())
235→                    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
236→                        parseException("The number is too large", iStart)
237→                    }
238→                    i += 1
239→                }
240→                number *= localSign
241→                if (i == text.length)
242→                    parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
243→                val wrongOrder = "Wrong component order: should be 'Y', 'M', 'W', 'D', then designator 'T', then 'H', 'M', 'S'"
244→                fun Long.toIntThrowing(component: Char): Int {
245→                    if (this < Int.MIN_VALUE || this > Int.MAX_VALUE)
246→                        parseException("Value $this does not fit into an Int, which is required for component '$component'", iStart)
247→                    return toInt()
248→                }
249→                when (text[i].uppercaseChar()) {
250→                    'Y' -> {
251→                        if (state >= AFTER_YEAR)
252→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
253→                        state = AFTER_YEAR
254→                        years = number.toIntThrowing('Y')
255→                    }
256→                    'M' -> {
257→                        if (state >= AFTER_T) {
258→                            // Minutes
259→                            if (state >= AFTER_MINUTE)
260→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
261→                            state = AFTER_MINUTE
262→                            minutes = number.toIntThrowing('M')
263→                        } else {
264→                            // Months
265→                            if (state >= AFTER_MONTH)
266→                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
267→                            state = AFTER_MONTH
268→                            months = number.toIntThrowing('M')
269→                        }
270→                    }
271→                    'W' -> {
272→                        if (state >= AFTER_WEEK)
273→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
274→                        state = AFTER_WEEK
275→                        weeks = number.toIntThrowing('W')
276→                    }
277→                    'D' -> {
278→                        if (state >= AFTER_DAY)
279→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
280→                        state = AFTER_DAY
281→                        days = number.toIntThrowing('D')
282→                    }
283→                    'H' -> {
284→                        if (state >= AFTER_HOUR || state < AFTER_T)
285→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
286→                        state = AFTER_HOUR
287→                        hours = number.toIntThrowing('H')
288→                    }
289→                    'S' -> {
290→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
291→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
292→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
293→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
294→                    }
295→                    '.', ',' -> {
296→                        i += 1
297→                        if (i >= text.length)
298→                            parseException("Expected designator 'S' after ${text[i - 1]}", i)
299→                        val iStartFraction = i
300→                        while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9')
301→                            i += 1
302→                        val fractionLength = i - iStartFraction
303→                        if (fractionLength > 9)
304→                            parseException("Only the nanosecond fractions of a second are supported", iStartFraction)
305→                        val fractionalPart = text.substring(iStartFraction, i) + "0".repeat(9 - fractionLength)
306→                        nanoseconds = fractionalPart.toInt(10) * localSign
307→                        if (text[i] != 'S')
308→                            parseException("Expected the 'S' designator after a fraction", i)
309→                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
310→                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
311→                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
312→                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
313→                    }
314→                    else -> parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
315→                }
316→                i += 1
317→           }
318→        }
319→    }
320→}

322→/**
323→ * @suppress
324→ */
325→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DateTimePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
326→public fun String.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = DateTimePeriod.parse(this)
327→
328→/**
329→ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores date components and has all time components equal to zero.
330→ *
331→ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
332→ * the time components are zero.
333→ *
334→ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate],
335→ * but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
336→ *
337→ * ### Usage
338→ *
339→ * - [parse]: Parses an ISO-8601 string (e.g. `P1Y2M`).
340→ * - Difference between dates:
341→ *   ```
342→ *   val period = date1.periodUntil(date2)
343→ *   ```
344→ */
345→@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
346→public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
347→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
348→    override val days: Int,
349→) : DateTimePeriod() {
350→    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
351→    // avoiding excessive computations
352→    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
353→    override val hours: Int get() = 0
354→
355→    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
356→    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
357→
358→    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
359→    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
360→
361→    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
362→    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
363→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0
364→
365→    public companion object {
366→        /**
367→         * Parses the ISO-8601 duration representation as a [DatePeriod].
368→         *
369→         * This function is equivalent to [DateTimePeriod.parse], but will fail if any of the time components are not
370→         * zero.
371→         *
372→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed, the boundaries of [DatePeriod] are exceeded,
373→         * or any time components are not zero.
374→         *
375→         * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
376→         */
377→        public fun parse(text: String): DatePeriod =
378→            when (val period = DateTimePeriod.parse(text)) {
379→                is DatePeriod -> period
380→                else -> throw DateTimeFormatException("Period $period (parsed from string $text) is not date-based")
381→            }
382→    }
383→}
384→
385→/**
386→ * @suppress
387→ */
388→@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DatePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
389→public fun String.toDatePeriod(): DatePeriod = DatePeriod.parse(this)
390→
391→private class DateTimePeriodImpl(
392→    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
393→    override val days: Int,
394→    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long,
395→) : DateTimePeriod()
396→
397→// TODO: these calculations fit in a JS Number. Possible to do an expect/actual here.
398→private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
399→    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
400→        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
401→        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
402→    }
403→
404→private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
405→    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
406→    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
407→    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
408→    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
409→    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
410→    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
411→    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
412→    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
413→    return try {
414→        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
415→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
416→        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
417→    }
418→}
419→
420→internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
421→    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)
422→        DateTimePeriodImpl(totalMonths, days, totalNanoseconds)
423→    else
424→        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)
425→
426→/**
427→ * Constructs a new [DateTimePeriod]. If all the time components are zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
428→ *
429→ * It is recommended to always explicitly name the arguments when constructing this manually,
430→ * like `DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 12)`.
431→ *
432→ * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
433→ * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2)`.
434→ *
435→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
436→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [hours], [minutes], [seconds] and [nanoseconds]
437→ * overflows a [Long].
438→ */
439→public fun DateTimePeriod(
440→    years: Int = 0,
441→    months: Int = 0,
442→    days: Int = 0,
443→    hours: Int = 0,
444→    minutes: Int = 0,
445→    seconds: Int = 0,
446→    nanoseconds: Long = 0
447→): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths(years, months), days,
448→    totalNanoseconds(hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds))
449→
450→/**
451→ * Constructs a [DateTimePeriod] from a [Duration].
452→ *
453→ * If the duration value is too big to be represented as a [Long] number of nanoseconds,
454→ * the result will be [Long.MAX_VALUE] nanoseconds.
455→ */
456→// TODO: maybe it's more consistent to throw here on overflow?
457→public fun Duration.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalNanoseconds = inWholeNanoseconds)
458→
459→/**
460→ * Adds two [DateTimePeriod] instances.
461→ *
462→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
463→ */
464→public operator fun DateTimePeriod.plus(other: DateTimePeriod): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(
465→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
466→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
467→    safeAdd(totalNanoseconds, other.totalNanoseconds),
468→)
469→
470→/**
471→ * Adds two [DatePeriod] instances.
472→ *
473→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
474→ */
475→public operator fun DatePeriod.plus(other: DatePeriod): DatePeriod = DatePeriod(
476→    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
477→    safeAdd(days, other.days),
478→)
479→
